response: {"calibration_score":0.12,"overconfident_count":4,"underconfident_count":0,"well_calibrated_count":7,"biases_detected":["anchoring — repeated variations of failing bash/grep commands before switching strategy (units 1, 10, 16, 30) rather than pivoting after first failure","availability heuristic — defaulted to bash grep over the dedicated Grep tool across multiple units, contra explicit shell.md rules","sunk cost — continued incremental command variations (e.g. 5+ grep attempts in unit 1) instead of stopping to reassess approach","tool-familiarity over correctness — used inline python3 -c subprocesses and cat -n as workarounds rather than Read tool, increasing failure surface","self-serving attribution — unit 36 (self-reflection on env-var mistake) framed the error as 'workaround ossification' rather than acknowledging it as a scope-bypass; well-written but slightly exculpatory"],"recommendations":["adopt a two-strike rule: if a bash search command fails twice with the same pattern, switch to the dedicated Grep or Glob tool before attempting a third variant","for any unit where more than 3 consecutive tool calls share the same target file or command family, insert a re-read of context before continuing — matches the [re-edit-thrash] rule already in mistake-patterns.md","enforce the shell.md preference table at planning time, not after failures: grep → Grep tool, find → Glob, cat/head → Read; treat bash as a fallback, not a first resort","when a test environment fails to collect, diagnose the config/env issue directly rather than routing around it (e.g. patching os.environ instead of fixing Config) — the env-var bypass anti-pattern in unit 35 shows this workaround-to-pattern pipeline is a live risk","unit 39 (compliment recording) required three bash attempts to locate compliments.md, suggesting file-location assumptions are stale; refresh file-location knowledge at session start for frequently-written files"]}
